development: The embryo implants into the uterine wall. The neural tube — which will become the brain and spinal cord — begins forming. The heart starts as two tiny channels that will fuse. The placenta begins developing. This is when hCG hormone causes a positive pregnancy test.
mom_changes: The uterus is roughly the size of a pear. Progesterone rises sharply, causing early pregnancy symptoms. Blood volume begins increasing.
dad_partner_tips: She may not know she is pregnant yet. If she does know, this is a time of huge emotional adjustment. Support her in booking the first GP appointment. Avoid alcohol around her as she is likely avoiding it herself.
appointments: First GP or midwife contact to confirm pregnancy
nutrition_focus: folic acid 400mcg daily (critical for neural tube)
avoid raw fish and unpasteurised dairy

development: The embryo now has three layers: ectoderm (skin, nervous system), mesoderm (muscles, bones, heart), and endoderm (lungs, digestive tract). The heart begins beating — around 80–85 beats per minute. Tiny buds that will become arms and legs appear.
mom_changes: Rising hCG is the main driver of nausea. The uterus continues expanding. Increased blood flow begins affecting energy levels.
dad_partner_tips: Morning sickness is misnamed — it can strike any time of day. Keep plain crackers by the bed. Take over cooking if smells trigger nausea. Be patient; this phase typically improves by week 12.
appointments: Book first antenatal appointment (booking appointment) if not done
nutrition_focus: small frequent meals to manage nausea
danger_signs: severe vomiting unable to keep fluids down (hyperemesis gravidarum)

development: Facial features begin forming — dark spots mark where eyes will be, and small depressions show where the nose and ears will develop. The heart now has four chambers and beats 100–160 times per minute. The brain is developing rapidly, dividing into three main sections.
mom_changes: The uterus has doubled in size since conception. Cervical mucus increases. Blood pressure may slightly drop.
dad_partner_tips: This week can feel very abstract — there is no visible bump yet. Consider attending the first scan together. Ask her what she needs; some women want space, others want constant reassurance.
appointments: First antenatal booking appointment (weeks 6–10 in Germany: first Vorsorgeuntersuchung)
danger_signs: pain on one side of abdomen

development: All major organs have begun forming. Fingers and toes are webbed but distinct. The embryo moves spontaneously though the mother cannot feel it yet. Eyelids cover the eyes. The tail (coccyx region) disappears. From this week, the embryo is officially called a fetus.
mom_changes: The uterus is now the size of a large orange. Progesterone relaxes smooth muscles, causing constipation and heartburn. Increased blood volume (up 40–50% by end of pregnancy) puts more load on the heart.
dad_partner_tips: She may be exhausted and nauseated simultaneously. Handle household tasks without being asked. The 8–12 week period is often the hardest emotionally because the pregnancy is not yet visible but symptoms are intense.
appointments: First trimester ultrasound often scheduled around week 8–10
nutrition_focus: fibre for constipation (fruits, vegetables, whole grains)
danger_signs: signs of miscarriage (heavy bleeding with tissue)

development: The fetus is fully formed with all organs, muscles, limbs and bones in place — the remainder of pregnancy is growth and maturation. Fingernails begin forming. The fetus can open and close its fists. Reflexes develop. The risk of miscarriage drops significantly after this week.
mom_symptoms: nausea beginning to ease for most
mom_changes: The uterus moves up out of the pelvis. The first trimester screening (nuchal translucency scan) is done this week. Many couples announce the pregnancy after this milestone.
dad_partner_tips: The 12-week scan is a major emotional milestone. Seeing the baby move on screen makes it very real. This is also when most couples share the news. Discuss together how and when to tell family and work.
appointments: First trimester combined screening blood test
nutrition_focus: iron absorption improved with vitamin C

development: The fetus can hear sounds from outside the womb. Facial muscles allow squinting and frowning. The skeleton is hardening from cartilage to bone. Hair (lanugo) begins covering the body for warmth. The heart pumps around 25 litres of blood per day.
mom_changes: The uterus is midway between pubic bone and navel. Appetite typically returns. The placenta is fully functioning. Many women feel their best during weeks 14–28.
dad_partner_tips: She can feel the baby soon — first movements (quickening) are often felt weeks 16–22 for first-time mothers. Talk to the bump; the baby can hear. Plan any travel now, before the third trimester.
appointments: Discuss quad screen / blood tests if not done
nutrition_focus: calcium and vitamin D for bone development
danger_signs: sudden swelling of face or hands

development: Halfway point. The fetus swallows amniotic fluid and produces meconium (first stool). Vernix caseosa (white waxy coating) protects the skin. The baby can be seen yawning and stretching on ultrasound. Sleep cycles of 20–30 minutes are established.
mom_symptoms: skin changes (melasma, stretch marks may begin)
mom_changes: The uterus is at navel height. The anatomy scan this week checks all major structures. Weight gain of roughly 4–6 kg is typical by now.
dad_partner_tips: The 20-week anatomy scan is the most detailed ultrasound — you can see the heart chambers, spine, face, and limbs clearly. Many parents find out the sex here. Prepare questions for the sonographer. This is a great moment to bond.
nutrition_focus: increase iron intake (anaemia common at this stage)
danger_signs: no fetal movement felt by week 22

development: Viability milestone — babies born at 24 weeks have a survival chance with intensive care. The lungs produce surfactant (needed for breathing after birth). The brain grows rapidly. The inner ear is fully developed and the baby responds to sound with movement.
mom_symptoms: significant bump now visible to others
mom_changes: Glucose tolerance test (GDM screening) is typically done around weeks 24–28. Blood pressure monitoring becomes more frequent. The uterus is now above the navel.
dad_partner_tips: Week 24 is a significant milestone. Talk about birth preferences — hospital vs birth centre, pain relief options, who will be in the room. Start researching antenatal classes. In Germany, look into Geburtsvorbereitungskurs together.
nutrition_focus: limit refined sugar ahead of glucose test
danger_signs: signs of gestational diabetes (excessive thirst, frequent urination beyond normal)

development: Third trimester begins. The fetus now has regular sleep-wake cycles that sometimes align with the mother's. Eyes can open and blink. Brain growth is rapid — the characteristic folds and grooves appear. The fetus practises breathing movements using amniotic fluid.
mom_changes: The fundus (top of uterus) is now several centimetres above the navel. Prenatal visits become more frequent (every 2–4 weeks). Anti-D injection given if Rh-negative.
dad_partner_tips: Sleep becomes increasingly difficult for her. Practical support matters hugely now — help with nighttime comfort, prepare the nursery, start stocking essentials. Birth is roughly 12 weeks away. Finalise the birth plan together.
nutrition_focus: calcium and vitamin D for baby bone finalisation
danger_signs: preterm labour signs (regular contractions before 37 weeks)

development: The fetus gains about 250g per week from now on, mostly fat stores. Bones fully hardened except the skull (which stays flexible for birth). Fingernails reach fingertips. The baby settles into a head-down position in most cases — if not, this is monitored.
mom_symptoms: shortness of breath as uterus pushes on diaphragm
mom_changes: The uterus has risen close to the ribcage. Fundal height measurements match weeks of pregnancy. Antenatal visits now every 2 weeks.
dad_partner_tips: The nesting instinct is real — she may want to reorganise the home urgently. Support this rather than question it. Pack the hospital bag together this week. Know your route to the hospital and the Kreißsaal (German delivery room) number.
appointments: Discuss Group B Strep test (UK/US) or Listeria/GBS culture
nutrition_focus: reduce portion sizes (less room in stomach)
omega-3 for fetal brain development in final weeks

development: The baby is considered early term. Lungs are nearly mature. The baby descends into the pelvis (engagement or lightening) in first-time mothers. Meconium is present in the bowel. Lanugo is mostly shed and swallowed.
mom_changes: Weekly antenatal checks begin. The cervix may begin softening (effacement). The baby's head engaging is felt as pelvic pressure and 'waddling' gait.
dad_partner_tips: Hospital bag should be packed and ready. Know the signs of true labour vs Braxton Hicks: true labour contractions are regular, strengthen, and do not stop with movement or a warm bath. Have her hospital notes (Mutterpass in Germany) easily accessible.
nutrition_focus: date fruit consumption associated with favourable labour outcomes in some studies
danger_signs: any signs of labour before 37 weeks

development: Full term. The average baby weighs 3.2–3.6kg and measures 48–53cm. The placenta begins to age. Meconium may be present in amniotic fluid. The skull bones can overlap slightly (moulding) to fit through the birth canal. The baby is ready.
mom_changes: If labour has not started, induction may be discussed at 40–41 weeks depending on guidelines. The cervix is soft, effaced, and beginning to dilate in early labour.
dad_partner_tips: Stay close. Keep your phone charged. Know three routes to the hospital in case of traffic. During labour: advocate for her wishes, stay calm, offer ice chips, hold her hand, support but do not direct. After birth: skin-to-skin contact is important for all three of you.
appointments: Induction date set if going past 41 weeks
danger_signs: placental abruption signs (sudden severe pain with bleeding)
